brown_plant_hopper.summary: Small brownish planthoppers that suck sap at the base of rice tillers, causing severe yellowing patches known as hopper burn.
symptoms_list: Yellowing of plants from the base upward
Circular dried 'hopper burn' patches in the field
Adults and nymphs visible at base of tillers when parted
Sooty mould growth from honeydew secretion
prevention: Avoid excessive nitrogen application (split doses recommended)
Maintain proper plant spacing (20×15 cm) to improve air circulation
Drain field for 3-4 days at first sighting to break the lifecycle
Plant resistant varieties such as IR-64 or Swarna Sub-1 where available
Regular field scouting twice a week during vulnerable stages
organic_solutions: Spray neem oil 5 ml/litre of water at 7-day intervals
Release Mirid bug (Cyrtorhinus lividipennis) as a biocontrol agent
Apply fish-oil rosin soap 25 g/litre as a contact spray
Use yellow sticky traps at 10 per acre to monitor populations
chemical_treatments: Imidacloprid 17.8 SL @ 0.5 ml/litre at threshold of 5-10 hoppers/hill
Buprofezin 25 SC @ 1.6 ml/litre for nymphal stages
Pymetrozine 50 WG @ 0.6 g/litre as a selective alternative
Always rotate insecticide groups to prevent resistance
irrigation_advice: Drain standing water for 3-4 days when infestation is detected
Avoid continuous deep flooding which favours BPH multiplication
Re-irrigate to a shallow 2-3 cm depth after drainage period
Synchronise irrigation with neighbours to reduce migration
emergency_actions: Immediately drain the field and apply systemic insecticide spray
Spot-spray hopper burn patches twice with a 5-day gap
Report to nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) for community-wide action
Avoid blanket spraying which kills beneficial Mirid bug predators

treatment.timing: Apply within 48 hours; repeat after 7 days if needed

stem_borer.summary: Larvae of yellow stem borer moths tunnel inside rice tillers, killing the central shoot (dead heart) at vegetative stage and producing whiteheads at panicle stage.
symptoms_list: Dead hearts (dried central shoots) during vegetative stage
Whiteheads (empty white panicles) at flowering stage
Frass and sawdust-like material at base of tiller
Small entry holes near the base of damaged stems
prevention: Clip the tips of seedlings before transplanting to remove egg masses
Plough crop stubble immediately after harvest to destroy diapausing larvae
Avoid late transplanting beyond mid-July for Kharif rice
Synchronise transplanting in the village to break the host cycle
Use light traps from transplanting onwards
organic_solutions: Install one pheromone trap per acre at 1 m above canopy
Release Trichogramma japonicum @ 50,000 per acre, 3 times at weekly intervals
Hand-pick and destroy egg masses observed during scouting
Spray neem seed kernel extract 5% as a deterrent
chemical_treatments: Chlorantraniliprole 18.5 SC @ 0.4 ml/litre at 30 days after transplanting
Cartap hydrochloride 4 G @ 10 kg/acre as a granular application
Fipronil 5 SC @ 2 ml/litre when dead heart % crosses threshold
Apply when egg masses exceed 1 per square metre
irrigation_advice: Maintain shallow standing water (3-5 cm) which reduces moth landing
Avoid alternate wetting-drying during peak moth emergence
Top-up irrigation immediately after granular insecticide use
Drain briefly before scheduled foliar spray for better deposit
emergency_actions: Apply granular insecticide within 48 hours if dead heart > 10%
Combine chemical with mass pheromone trapping for community-level impact
Coordinate with adjacent farms to break the larval supply line
Notify the block agriculture officer for area-wide advisory

aphids.summary: Soft-bodied insects that form dense colonies on shoots and inflorescences, weakening plants and transmitting viral diseases.
symptoms_list: Leaf curling and yellowing in patches
Sticky honeydew coating on lower leaves
Sooty mould turning leaves black
Stunted growth and small pod / grain size
Visible clusters of green / black insects on shoots
prevention: Time sowing to avoid peak aphid migration (early-November for mustard)
Maintain field hygiene by removing weed hosts like Chenopodium
Encourage ladybird beetle populations by avoiding broad-spectrum sprays
Use yellow sticky traps at 8-10 per acre for monitoring
Mix nitrogen with potash to harden tissues
organic_solutions: Spray neem oil 3-5 ml/litre at first sighting
Release Coccinella septempunctata (ladybird) @ 1500 per acre
Strong water jet to dislodge colonies on hardy crops
Soap solution 1% as a contact wash
chemical_treatments: Thiamethoxam 25 WG @ 0.2 g/litre when threshold (20 aphids / plant) is crossed
Imidacloprid 17.8 SL @ 0.3 ml/litre as foliar spray
Dimethoate 30 EC @ 1.7 ml/litre for severe outbreak
Time sprays before bee activity (late afternoon)
irrigation_advice: Avoid water stress, which makes plants more susceptible
Light surface irrigation can dislodge colonies in dryland mustard
Synchronise irrigation with spray operations for better systemic uptake
emergency_actions: Apply systemic insecticide within 72 hours if colony density > 20 / plant
Issue ward-level advisory to neighbouring fields
Combine ladybird release with selective insecticide for sustained control
Re-scout 5 days after spray; repeat if needed

treatment.timing: At first sign of colony buildup

pod_borer.summary: Polyphagous caterpillar that bores into developing pods and feeds on seeds, causing major yield losses in pulses.
symptoms_list: Circular bore holes on pods
Larvae visible inside opened pods
Faecal pellets near pod entry holes
Premature drying and shedding of flowers and pods
Patches of damaged pods at field margins
prevention: Intercrop with coriander or marigold as a trap and repellent
Install bird perches at 30 per acre to encourage natural predation
Avoid continuous pulse-only cropping; rotate with cereals
Time sowing so pod-formation does not coincide with peak moth flight
Monitor with pheromone traps from flowering onwards
organic_solutions: HaNPV (Helicoverpa nuclear polyhedrosis virus) @ 250 LE per acre at dusk
Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) kurstaki @ 1 g/litre on young larvae
Neem seed kernel extract 5% at flowering
Trap crops of marigold or okra at field borders
chemical_treatments: Emamectin benzoate 5 SG @ 0.4 g/litre on young larvae
Indoxacarb 14.5 SC @ 1 ml/litre when threshold (1 larva / metre row) is crossed
Spinosad 45 SC @ 0.3 ml/litre as a low-residue option
Spray in early morning or late evening for best larval contact
irrigation_advice: Provide light irrigation at flowering to reduce moisture stress
Avoid over-irrigation which prolongs vegetative phase and exposes pods longer
Keep ridges dry to discourage larval pupation
emergency_actions: Spray Emamectin benzoate within 48 hours of crossing economic threshold
Combine HaNPV with bird perches for non-chemical knockdown
Hand-pick mature larvae at dusk from severely affected patches
Notify pulse value-chain stakeholders if outbreak is district-wide

army_worm.summary: Invasive caterpillar that scrapes maize leaves and bores into whorls, causing characteristic ragged holes and severe yield loss.
symptoms_list: Window-pane like scraping on younger leaves
Ragged holes and tattered upper leaves
Frass (insect droppings) inside the whorl
Larvae visible in maize whorls during morning hours
Stunted growth in seedling stage
prevention: Avoid staggered planting; sow within a 10-day window in the village
Inter-crop with legumes (cowpea, pigeonpea) to disrupt host search
Deep summer ploughing to expose pupae to sun and predators
Install pheromone traps (FAW lure) at 5 per acre
Encourage natural enemies by reducing broad-spectrum sprays
organic_solutions: Apply sand + ash mixture (9:1) into the whorl as a mechanical barrier
Spray neem seed kernel extract 5% in the whorl
Release Trichogramma chilonis @ 50,000 per acre
Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) @ 1 g/litre directed into whorls
chemical_treatments: Spinetoram 11.7 SC @ 0.5 ml/litre directed into whorl
Chlorantraniliprole 18.5 SC @ 0.4 ml/litre whorl spray
Emamectin benzoate 5 SG @ 0.4 g/litre when threshold (10% damaged whorls)
Avoid spraying during silking to protect pollinators
irrigation_advice: Avoid water stress during vegetative phase; stressed maize attracts more larvae
Irrigate adequately to support recovery after damage
Do not flood-irrigate immediately after granular treatment to preserve activity
emergency_actions: Spot-treat infected whorls within 24-48 hours of detection
Hand-pick large larvae and destroy egg masses
Coordinate with block agriculture department for area-wide spray window
Activate community FAW monitoring teams
